description: Model asset prices using the lognormal distribution — bounded below by zero, right-skewed, and the theoretical consequence of normally distributed continuously compounded returns

fields.name: mu
fields.label: Mean of the associated normal distribution (i.e., of ln Y)

fields.name: sigma
fields.label: Standard deviation of the associated normal distribution

fields.name: compute
fields.label: mean | variance | pdf | cdf | quantile | all

core_formulas.relationship: Y is lognormal if and only if ln(Y) is normal with parameters (mu, sigma^2)
core_formulas.pdf: f(y) = (1 / (y * sigma * sqrt(2*pi))) * exp(-(ln(y) - mu)^2 / (2 * sigma^2)) for y > 0

properties.bounded_below: Y > 0 always — matches the reality that asset prices cannot go negative
properties.multiplicative: Product of independent lognormals is lognormal; log-additive under compounding

parameter_semantics.two_parameter_sets: Track both (mu, sigma) of ln(Y) and (E(Y), Var(Y)) of Y — they differ by an exp(0.5*sigma^2) factor
parameter_semantics.factor_note: E(Y) > exp(mu) because variance spreads the distribution upward but floors it at 0

investment_applications.option_pricing: Black-Scholes-Merton assumes underlying asset is lognormally distributed
investment_applications.risk_management: Scenario distribution of future prices from current price + normal returns
investment_applications.monte_carlo: Generate terminal asset prices via P_T = P_0 * exp(r_0,T)

validation.sigma_positive: sigma > 0 (zero sigma collapses to a point mass)

compute_moments.description: Compute mean and variance of Y from (mu, sigma)

errors.message: Both mu and sigma are required

anti_patterns.rule: Do not confuse the (mu, sigma) of ln(Y) with the (E(Y), sd(Y)) of Y itself
anti_patterns.why: The parameters describe the associated normal distribution; the moments of Y require the exp(mu + 0.5*sigma^2) transform. Mixing them produces wildly wrong option prices and VaR estimates

anti_patterns.rule: Do not use the lognormal to model arithmetic returns
anti_patterns.why: Arithmetic returns can be negative; lognormal is strictly positive. Use lognormal for prices and simple/compounded gross returns (1 + r), not for returns themselves

anti_patterns.rule: Do not assume lognormal parameters are stable across regimes
anti_patterns.why: Realized volatility clusters; sigma in calm markets dramatically understates tail prices in crises. Use regime-switching or stochastic volatility models for stress analysis

anti_patterns.rule: Do not use the lognormal for returns with heavy tails or skew
anti_patterns.why: Lognormal is determined by its first two moments; fat-tailed distributions (e.g., equity returns) need t-distribution or jump-diffusion models for accurate tail risk

goals.description: Model asset prices using the lognormal distribution — bounded below by zero, right-skewed, and the theoretical consequence of normally distributed continuously compounded returns

success_metrics.measurement: All decisions have complete audit trails

constraints.description: All operations must be auditable and traceable

tradeoffs.reason: trading operations require precise execution and full audit trails

invariants: error messages never expose internal system details

worked_example.interpretation: Normal returns with mean 7% and sigma 12% produce lognormal future prices with mean ~1.08x current

black_scholes_context.assumption: S_T is lognormal with parameters (ln(S_0) + (r - 0.5*sigma^2)*T, sigma^2 * T)
black_scholes_context.usage: Closed-form European option prices assume this distribution of terminal asset price
